instructions:
Build the iOS analog of the TS / Flutter / Android doc-as-test frameworks. Final platform of the four. Same value model: extract ```swift code blocks from high-traffic docs, type-check against the real AmitySDK iOS source, report pass/fail/skip.

New subdirectory: `.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/` (sibling to ts/flutter/android frameworks).

## Part A — Pick the simplest toolchain

Four real options; pick whichever Copilot can get working fastest. Document the choice + tradeoff in README. The host machine (per env) is darwin so swiftc is available — assume macOS is acceptable for v1; CI considerations are a follow-up.

  **Option A (preferred — Swift Package Manager + local path)**: a minimal `Package.swift` declaring AmitySDK as a binary target via the AmitySDK xcframework at `AmitySDKIOS/AmitySDK.xcframework` (or wherever it lives — investigate the SDK repo layout). Use `swift build --target docs-ops-snippets` to type-check.

  **Option B (cleanest if SPM is hard — official SwiftPM repo)**: Package.swift depending on the published SwiftPM repo `https://github.com/AmityCo/Amity-Social-Cloud-SDK-iOS-SwiftPM` (referenced in the iOS quickstart doc). This means testing against the PUBLISHED SDK rather than the live source. Acceptable for v1.

  **Option C (lightest manual — `swiftc -typecheck`)**: build the SDK once to get module files, then `swiftc -typecheck -I <module-path> -F <framework-path> file.swift` per block. Faster per-run but more fiddly setup.

  **Option D (last resort — Xcode project)**: full xcodebuild against an xcframework-linked project. Slowest, most fragile. Only if A/B/C all fail.

## Part B — Preamble: wrap each block as a function body

Swift allows top-level functions in scripts (`main.swift`) but library targets require a containing scope. Wrap each block in a global function:

```swift
// preamble.swift — context for doc snippets. The extractor wraps each block in
// a function with these parameters in scope.
import AmitySDK

@available(iOS 14.0, *)
func docSnippet(
    apiKey: String = "",
    region: AmityRegion = .sg,
    userId: String = "",
    displayName: String = "",
    channelId: String = "",
    subChannelId: String = "",
    messageId: String = "",
    postId: String = "",
    communityId: String = "",
    commentId: String = "",
    pollId: String = "",
    fileId: String = "",
    client: AmityClient? = nil,
    channel: AmityChannel? = nil,
    message: AmityMessage? = nil,
    post: AmityPost? = nil,
    comment: AmityComment? = nil,
    community: AmityCommunity? = nil
) async throws {
    // <SNIPPET_BODY> (extractor inserts each block here)
}
```

Verify each type against `.docs-ops/sdk-surface/ios.json` before declaring. If a type isn't there (e.g., the iOS SDK uses a different name), drop it from the preamble and note in the run report. Conservative > false-negative.

The `async throws` wrapper handles the most common iOS pattern (async APIs that can throw). Snippets that don't use async/throw will still compile inside an async-throwing context.

## Part C — Page list (`pages.json`)

Reuse the same high-traffic page set as Flutter/Android. Add `ios-quick-start.mdx` to a `ios_specific` section since it's the iOS entry point. Resolve `enumerate:` entries at extract time.

## Part D — Block extractor (`extract-blocks.py`)

Mirror Flutter/Android extractor structure. Differences:
  - Extract ```swift blocks only (not ```objc / ```objective-c in v1 — handle later if needed).
  - For each block, wrap in the `docSnippet` async throws function with context vars in scope.
  - Honor `<!-- doc-as-test: skip -->` markers.
  - Naming: `<page-slug>-block-<N>.swift`.

## Part E — Test runner (`run-tests.py`)

Depending on toolchain chosen in Part A:
  - SPM: `swift build --target docs-ops-snippets 2>&1`, parse errors.
  - swiftc: `swiftc -typecheck -I <module-path> <file>.swift`, capture stderr per file.
  - Either way: errors mapped to source blocks via the file naming.

Gate policy mirrors prior platforms:
  - severity=error → BLOCKING
  - severity=warning → WARNING (non-blocking)
  - note/remark → ignore

Swift compiler errors look like: `error: cannot find 'foo' in scope`, `error: extra argument 'bar' in call`, etc. Capture both the error code (if present) and the message.

Emit `results/latest.json` with the same schema as TS/Flutter/Android doc-as-test.

## Part F — README + .gitignore + first run

`README.md`: brief — which toolchain you picked + why, host requirements (macOS for swiftc), how to run locally, where the report lives, note that CI may need Mac runners for this to work in GitHub Actions later.

Proactively update top-level `.gitignore` to ignore generated artifacts (avoid the leak that happened with Flutter + Android):
```
# iOS doc-as-test generated files
.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/results/extracted/*.swift
.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/results/extracted/_manifest.json
.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/.build/
.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/.swiftpm/
.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/Package.resolved
```

Keep `.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/results/extracted/.gitkeep` so the directory persists.

RUN the framework end-to-end. Expected first-run pass rate: 60-85% (similar to Flutter/Android). Don't fix failures in this task; report them in notes.

## Out of scope

- Wiring into `check-drift.py` — separate follow-up (0059, same shape as 0055/0057 wired Flutter/Android).
- Fixing failing blocks — iterative cleanup is a follow-up task.
- Objective-C blocks — TS only in v1 (sorry — they're rare in social.plus docs anyway).
- CI / Mac-runner setup — Phase 2.2 territory.

## Success criteria (LOOSE — framework build, not a quality gate)

- Framework runs end-to-end on this Mac.
- `results/latest.json` produced with valid schema.
- At least 30 Swift blocks extracted.
- At least one block PASSES.

Report in `notes`: which toolchain you picked + why; first-run stats; top failure reasons; preamble gaps vs real doc drift estimate; any iOS-specific quirks (e.g., MainActor isolation, Combine vs async/await mixing, ObjC interop in Swift blocks).

validators.name: ios_framework_runs_and_produces_valid_report
validators.command: test -f .docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/results/latest.json && python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.docs-ops/integration-tests/ios/results/latest.json')); assert 'stats' in d and 'failures' in d; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_extracted',0)>=30, f'too few blocks: {d[\"stats\"].get(\"blocks_extracted\")}'; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_passed',0)>=1, 'zero blocks passed — toolchain likely broken'; print('OK')"
